content: I've been experimenting with sourdough bread baking lately. My first few attempts were pretty dense, but I think I'm getting the hang of it now.
That's great! Sourdough can be tricky at first. What kind of flour are you using for your starter?

I started with all-purpose flour but switched to a mix of bread flour and whole wheat. The whole wheat seems to give it more character. I've been doing this on weekends since my MBA classes take up so much time during the week.
The bread flour and whole wheat combination sounds perfect. How's the MBA program going? Must be challenging to balance with work and baking!

It's intense, especially with my physical therapy practice here in Phoenix. I'm specializing in sports injuries, so my schedule can get pretty packed with athletes. But I've found that baking is actually a nice stress reliever after long days.
Working with athletes sounds fascinating! Do you find that many of them are interested in nutrition and cooking as part of their training?

Absolutely. I actually had a professional soccer player last month who was really into making his own fermented foods. We talked about kimchi and sauerkraut for gut health. He was amazed when I mentioned I'd been trying sourdough too.
That's such an interesting connection! Have you tried incorporating any of your other hobbies into your cooking experiments?

Funny you should ask. I've been thinking about using my archery skills to hunt for wild yeast sources, but I'm not sure if that's how it works. Mostly I just use the air here in Phoenix, which seems pretty active.

Hunting for wild yeast with archery is creative thinking, but I think you'll have better luck with the traditional method! The desert air in Phoenix probably does have some unique characteristics though.

Yeah, I was mostly joking about the archery part. Though I did take my starter on a hiking trip to the Superstition Mountains last month. The elevation change seemed to really boost its activity for a few days.
Taking your starter hiking is dedication! Did you bake at the campsite or wait until you got back to Phoenix?

I couldn't resist - I made a small loaf right there at the base camp. It wasn't my best work, but there's something magical about fresh bread in the mountains. I think I'll try bringing some coffee brewing gear next time too.

That sounds absolutely perfect - fresh sourdough and coffee in the mountains. You're living the dream! Have you thought about teaching a workshop that combines your PT knowledge with baking? The posture and movement aspects could be really valuable.
